quiz_questions.question_text: What is a physical quantity?

options.text: A property that can be measured with a number and a unit
A mathematical formula used in physics
An abstract concept with no measurement
A property that can only be observed qualitatively

quiz_questions.explanation: A physical quantity is any property of a material or system that can be quantified — it always consists of a numerical value and an associated unit.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many SI base quantities are there?

options.text: Seven
Five
Six
Ten

quiz_questions.explanation: The International System of Units defines exactly seven base quantities: length, mass, time, electric current, temperature, amount of substance, and luminous intensity.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the SI base unit for mass?

options.text: Kilogram (kg)
Gram (g)
Pound (lb)
Newton (N)

quiz_questions.explanation: The kilogram (kg) is the SI base unit for mass. It is the only SI base unit that includes a prefix in its name.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a derived quantity?

options.text: Speed
Length
Mass
Time

quiz_questions.explanation: Speed is derived from the base quantities of length and time (speed = distance/time). Length, mass, and time are all base quantities.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the SI base unit for time?

options.text: Second (s)
Minute (min)
Hour (h)
Millisecond (ms)

quiz_questions.explanation: The second (s) is the SI base unit for time. Minutes, hours, and milliseconds are not SI base units.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the SI base unit for electric current?

options.text: Ampere (A)
Volt (V)
Ohm (Ω)
Coulomb (C)

quiz_questions.explanation: The ampere (A) is the SI base unit for electric current. Volts measure potential difference, ohms measure resistance, and coulombs measure charge.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the SI base unit for temperature?

options.text: Kelvin (K)
Celsius (°C)
Fahrenheit (°F)
Joule (J)

quiz_questions.explanation: The kelvin (K) is the SI base unit for thermodynamic temperature. Celsius and Fahrenheit are commonly used but are not SI base units.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a base quantity in the SI system?

options.text: Amount of substance
Force
Pressure
Energy

quiz_questions.explanation: Amount of substance (measured in moles) is one of the seven SI base quantities. Force, pressure, and energy are all derived quantities.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens when you add two values with different units?

options.text: The addition is physically meaningless
The units cancel out
You always get the larger unit
The result takes the unit of the first value

quiz_questions.explanation: Adding quantities with different units (e.g., 5 meters + 3 seconds) is physically meaningless. Only quantities with the same dimensions can be added or subtracted.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a derived unit?

options.text: Newton (N)
Kilogram (kg)
Second (s)
Kelvin (K)

quiz_questions.explanation: The newton is a derived unit defined as kg·m/s². Kilogram, second, and kelvin are all SI base units.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a physical quantity?

options.text: A measurable property of a physical system expressed as a numerical value multiplied by a unit
Any number used in a physics equation
A qualitative observation about a physical system
A unit of measurement defined by the SI system

quiz_questions.explanation: A physical quantity consists of two parts: a numerical value and a unit. For example, a length of 5.0 meters has the numerical value 5.0 and the unit meters.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a base quantity?

options.text: A fundamental physical quantity that cannot be expressed in terms of other quantities and serves as a building block for all other measurements
Any quantity that is commonly used in everyday life
A quantity defined by a mathematical formula
A measurement that uses only whole numbers

quiz_questions.explanation: Base quantities are the seven fundamental measurements (length, mass, time, current, temperature, amount of substance, luminous intensity) from which all other quantities are derived.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a derived quantity?

options.text: A physical quantity defined by combining base quantities through multiplication, division, or other mathematical operations
A quantity that exists independently of any other quantity
A measurement that can only be estimated
A quantity that uses non-SI units

quiz_questions.explanation: Derived quantities are formed by combining base quantities. For example, velocity (m/s) combines length and time, and force (kg·m/s²) combines mass, length, and time.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the SI system?

options.text: The International System of Units — a globally agreed-upon set of standard base units used for scientific measurement
A system of measurement used exclusively in Europe
Any system that measures in metric units
A collection of mathematical equations for physics

quiz_questions.explanation: The SI system (Système International d'Unités) provides seven base units that form the worldwide standard for scientific measurement and international commerce.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes dimensional consistency?

options.text: The requirement that both sides of a physics equation must have the same fundamental dimensions
The requirement that all measurements use the same unit system
The rule that equations must be balanced numerically
The rule that physical constants must have no units

quiz_questions.explanation: Dimensional consistency means that the dimensions on both sides of an equation must match. An equation like distance = speed × time satisfies this: [L] = [L/T] × [T] = [L].

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a unit?

options.text: A standard reference quantity used to express the magnitude of a physical measurement
A number assigned to a physical property
The smallest possible measurement of a quantity
A mathematical symbol in an equation

quiz_questions.explanation: A unit is a defined standard of measurement. For example, the meter is the unit for length, providing a reference so that all length measurements are consistent and comparable.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes luminous intensity?

options.text: The power of light emitted by a source in a particular direction, measured in candelas
The total energy output of a light source
The speed at which light travels through a medium
The frequency of visible light waves

quiz_questions.explanation: Luminous intensity is one of the seven SI base quantities. It measures the wavelength-weighted power emitted by a light source in a specific direction, with the SI unit candela (cd).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the mole?

options.text: The SI unit for amount of substance, defined as exactly 6.022 × 10²³ elementary entities
A unit of mass used for very small particles
The number of atoms in one gram of any element
A unit of volume for measuring gases

quiz_questions.explanation: The mole is the SI base unit for amount of substance. One mole contains exactly 6.02214076 × 10²³ elementary entities (Avogadro's number), whether atoms, molecules, or other particles.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a fundamental constant?

options.text: A physical quantity whose numerical value is fixed by the laws of nature and does not change
A variable that appears in every physics equation
A mathematical number like π or e
Any quantity that scientists have agreed to keep unchanged

quiz_questions.explanation: Fundamental constants like the speed of light (c), Planck's constant (h), and the gravitational constant (G) have fixed values determined by nature, not by human convention.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the kelvin?

options.text: The SI base unit of thermodynamic temperature, defined by fixing the numerical value of the Boltzmann constant
A unit of temperature equal to one degree Celsius
The average kinetic energy of gas molecules
A unit of thermal energy

quiz_questions.explanation: The kelvin is the SI base unit for temperature. Its scale starts at absolute zero (0 K), where all thermal motion ceases. One kelvin increment equals one degree Celsius increment.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student measures the length of a table as '1.52.' Their teacher says the measurement is incomplete. What is MOST LIKELY missing from this measurement?

options.text: The unit — a measurement must include both a numerical value and a unit to have physical meaning
The decimal place — they need more digits
The temperature — measurements depend on thermal expansion
The student's name — measurements must be attributed

quiz_questions.explanation: A physical quantity requires both a numerical value and a unit. '1.52' alone conveys no physical meaning — it could be meters, feet, or any other unit. The measurement should be written as 1.52 m (or the appropriate unit).

quiz_questions.question_text: An engineer needs to calculate the area of a rectangular plot that is 50 m long and 30 m wide. She expresses the area in m². This unit (m²) is an example of:

options.text: A derived unit — formed by multiplying the base unit of length by itself
A base unit — because meters are a base unit
A dimensionless quantity — because it is just a number
A fundamental constant — because area formulas never change

quiz_questions.explanation: Area (m²) is a derived quantity obtained from the base quantity length. Since area = length × width, its unit is m × m = m², which is derived from the SI base unit meter.

quiz_questions.question_text: A physics student writes the equation: distance = velocity + time. A classmate says this equation cannot be correct without even checking the numbers. How does the classmate know?

options.text: The dimensions don't match — you cannot add velocity [L/T] and time [T] because they have different dimensions
The equation uses the wrong variables
Velocity is always larger than time
The equation is missing a constant

quiz_questions.explanation: Dimensional analysis reveals the error immediately. Velocity has dimensions [L/T] and time has dimensions [T]. Since you cannot add quantities with different dimensions, the equation is dimensionally inconsistent and therefore physically invalid.

quiz_questions.question_text: A scientist measures temperature in kelvin, mass in kilograms, and time in seconds. All three of these belong to which category?

options.text: SI base quantities — each is one of the seven fundamental measurements that cannot be derived from others
Derived quantities — each is calculated from simpler quantities
Imperial units — they belong to the British measurement system
Dimensionless constants — they have no physical dimensions

quiz_questions.explanation: Temperature (K), mass (kg), and time (s) are three of the seven SI base quantities. They are defined independently and serve as the foundation from which all other physical quantities are derived.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a laboratory, a researcher expresses force in units of kg·m/s². This combination of base units is given the special name 'newton.' What does this tell us about force?

options.text: Force is a derived quantity — it is defined through base quantities of mass, length, and time via Newton's second law
Force is a base quantity given a simpler name for convenience
Force cannot be measured directly
Force is dimensionless because the units cancel out

quiz_questions.explanation: The newton (N = kg·m/s²) is a derived unit. It combines three base units, reflecting that force arises from the relationship F = ma. Many derived units receive special names for convenience.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student claims that energy and torque must be the same physical quantity because they both have units of N·m. Is this reasoning correct?

options.text: No — although they share the same dimensions, energy is a scalar and torque is a vector, making them fundamentally different quantities
Yes — any quantities with the same units are identical
No — energy is measured in joules, not newton-meters
Yes — dimensional analysis proves they are interchangeable

quiz_questions.explanation: While energy and torque both have dimensions [M L² T⁻²] and the unit N·m, they describe different physical concepts. Energy (a scalar) measures capacity to do work, while torque (a vector) measures rotational force. Dimensional analysis alone cannot distinguish between quantities of the same dimensions.

quiz_questions.question_text: NASA engineers must ensure that all international partners use consistent units. In 1999, a Mars orbiter was lost because one team used pounds while another used newtons. This disaster highlights the importance of:

options.text: A universal unit system (SI) — inconsistent units between teams led to a navigation error that destroyed the spacecraft
Using larger spacecraft to survive errors
Avoiding international collaborations in space missions
Rounding all measurements to whole numbers

quiz_questions.explanation: The Mars Climate Orbiter was lost due to a unit mismatch: Lockheed Martin used imperial pound-force·seconds while NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory expected SI newton·seconds. This $327.6 million loss underscores why the SI system exists.

quiz_questions.question_text: A physics textbook lists the speed of light as 3.00 × 10⁸ m/s and the charge of an electron as 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C. Both of these are examples of:

options.text: Physical quantities with defined numerical values and specific SI units
Derived quantities because they are calculated from other measurements
Approximate guesses that change over time
Dimensionless numbers that have no units

quiz_questions.explanation: Both the speed of light and the elementary charge are physical quantities — they have numerical values and SI units. They are also fundamental constants whose values are now fixed by definition in the modern SI system.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student converts the mass of a car from 1500 kg to grams and gets 1,500,000 g. Despite the different number, the physical quantity has not changed. Why?

options.text: The physical quantity (mass) remains the same because changing units only changes the numerical value, not the actual amount of matter
The mass has actually increased by a factor of 1000
Grams and kilograms measure different physical quantities
The conversion is incorrect

quiz_questions.explanation: A physical quantity is invariant under unit conversion. 1500 kg and 1,500,000 g represent the same mass — only the numerical value and unit change, while the physical property itself remains identical.

quiz_questions.question_text: A researcher publishes a paper stating that a sample length is '12.5' without specifying any unit. A reviewer rejects this claim. Why is the reviewer justified?

options.text: A number without a unit has no physical meaning — it could represent 12.5 meters, centimeters, or any other unit, making the measurement ambiguous
The number 12.5 is too imprecise to be useful
Published papers must always use imperial units
The reviewer prefers a different number format

quiz_questions.explanation: In physics, every measurement must include a unit. A bare number like 12.5 is meaningless — the unit defines the scale and physical context. This principle is fundamental to clear scientific communication.
